title: Offices of Certified Public Accountants

narrative.executive_view: A lower-middle-market CPA firm is a credible AI value-migration target, but the investment is stronger as a regulated, relationship-led compliance and advisory platform than as a pure labor-arbitrage bet. The task map implies substantial exposure in workpaper preparation, tax preparation, bookkeeping, document assembly and administration, while licensed sign-off, independence, professional judgment and client responsibility remain human. Published transaction evidence supports buying a normalized operator near 4.5x EBITDA and, if it becomes a scaled platform with management depth and advisory mix, an exit nearer 8.5x [S4][S5]. The largest underwriting risk is that faster production eventually lowers benchmarked fees, especially in commodity tax and CAS; the counterweight is a scarce CPA labor pool, mandatory attest work and evidence that tax-firm respondents currently expect more rate increases than decreases [S1][S6].
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI should first compress preparation rather than eliminate the accountable professional. Tax research and return preparation, accounting and bookkeeping, document summarization and drafting are already prominent use cases among active tax, accounting and audit users [S1]. The within-role judgments therefore put the highest fractions on clerical, bookkeeping, tax-preparer, billing and administrative work, with a lower but still meaningful fraction for accountants and auditors because tie-outs, sampling support, workpaper drafting and technical research coexist with walkthroughs, skepticism and opinion responsibility. Financial and general managers retain pricing, client and review accountability. The residual is material because the supplied OEWS mix is at four-digit 5412, not CPA offices alone; it is treated as a mixed IT, HR, specialist and support tail rather than assumed fully automatable.
narrative.value_capture: The base case retains about three quarters of gross production savings before moat, then discounts again for competition and service mix. That is deliberately below full capture: time-based and hours-anchored fixed fees expose commodity preparation savings to renegotiation, self-service and client benchmarking. Yet the 2025 Thomson Reuters survey found 43% of tax, accounting and audit firm respondents expected rates to rise because of GenAI versus 39% expecting no change, and tax firms were comparatively willing to pass AI costs to clients [S1]. Attest licensing and independence create a durable access barrier [S6], while recurring client relationships and switching friction support price. These channels justify moderate retention, not immunity from deflation, and they are kept separate from the terminal-demand floor.
narrative.adoption_timing: Organizational adoption is real but far from effective saturation. Thomson Reuters reported 21% of tax, accounting and audit organizations already using GenAI in early 2025, up from 8% in 2024, while much of the market remained in planning or consideration [S1]. I carry 21% as the observed starting point and estimate three years from the run date to reach 50% effective adoption. The bridge allows for vendor-embedded tools and a labor shortage to accelerate uptake, but also for confidentiality, hallucination, workflow integration, review controls and busy-season implementation to slow conversion from experimentation into realized hours. Consolidation activity and partner age do not enter this adoption estimate.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The buyability case is favorable but competitive. The deterministic dataset identifies 2,286 estimated firms in the $1–10M EBITDA band. The Rosenberg succession material reports 22% of partners over 60 and 73% of firms expecting succession issues within ten years, with 57% of firms under $8M lacking a succession plan [S2]. KPMG identified 11 of the 30 largest U.S. accounting firms as PE-backed by Q3 2025 and describes rapid buy-and-build activity in a market of roughly 46,000–52,200 CPA firms [S3]. I use those 11 visible platforms as a conservative consolidator proxy. Buy and exit values are on adjusted EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation: the AICPA/Aon guide reports 2.99–4.45x average EBITDA, while current transaction analysis places traditional mid-size firms at 4.0–5.5x and PE platforms at 7.0–10.0x+ [S4][S5].
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal paid demand is durable in aggregate but uneven by revenue line. Only licensed CPA firms can provide attest services, and APS structures preserve the attest firm as a distinct, CPA-controlled entity subject to independence requirements [S6]. Audit opinions, lender and regulatory assurance, complex tax positions and accountable advice therefore survive even if staffing shrinks. Tax compliance and CAS have a weaker floor: platform-native automation and client self-service can reduce preparation hours and pressure simpler engagements. The likely endpoint is a smaller-headcount, higher-review-intensity firm rather than disappearance of the operator. This demand floor supports the terminal class; it is not used to increase capture or task replaceability.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are effective adoption timing, the fee response and multiple transferability. The adoption survey spans tax, accounting and audit organizations across several countries and sizes rather than U.S. lower-middle-market NAICS 541211 alone [S1]. The supplied role mix is broader four-digit 5412 and partner draws sit outside QCEW wages, so the deterministic labor share may understate true owner labor while the occupation mix may overstate clerical roles for CPA offices. The active-consolidator count is a conservative top-30 platform proxy, not an exhaustive census. Small-practice valuation sources mix EBITDA, SDE and revenue conventions; every multiple here assumes owner compensation is replaced with market pay. Finally, attest independence can limit cross-selling and APS regulation can constrain deal structure even while it protects terminal demand [S6].

occupations.title: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $263,807 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541211); class <5: 35,096 firms, avg revenue/firm $300,013 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $46,952 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 9,158 firms, avg revenue/firm $944,405 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $147,799 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 4,257 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,000,227 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $313,036 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,015 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,602,856 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,033,347 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 271 firms, avg revenue/firm $37,129,554 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,810,775 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 88 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,137,349,114 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $177,995,136 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 2,286
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Task-level estimates emphasize preparation, reconciliation, drafting and administration while preserving licensed sign-off, skepticism, client judgment and relationship work. Thomson Reuters documents current use in tax research, return preparation, bookkeeping, summarization and drafting; Census fixes the 541211 boundary.
caveats: The source survey spans tax, accounting and audit organizations rather than only U.S. NAICS 541211 firms.
The supplied role mix is four-digit 5412 and blends tax preparation, payroll and other accounting services; RESIDUAL is a large mixed tail.
Partner draws are largely outside QCEW wages, so wage weights understate owner economics and licensed review work.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Workpaper, tie-out, sampling support, return preparation and memo drafting automate; skepticism, walkthroughs, materiality and signing remain human.
Reconciliation, write-up, coding and close support are highly exposed, with exception handling and client data cleanup remaining.
Form-driven preparation and research automate substantially; review, taxpayer fact resolution and signature responsibility migrate upward.
Internal reporting and review routing automate, but partner economics, pricing, risk acceptance and client responsibility persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Scheduling, engagement letters, assembly and communications are mostly automatable; sensitive coordination persists.
Workflow reporting and resource planning automate while operating judgment, people leadership and client escalation remain.
Document handling, e-file transmittal and routine office processing are strongly exposed, subject to exception control.
Research, analysis drafts and recurring models augment well; bespoke advice, persuasion and accountable recommendations remain.
Time capture, invoice generation, collections prompts and posting are highly automatable; disputes remain human.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Queue allocation and quality flags automate, but supervision, coaching and exception ownership persist.
The unlisted tail mixes automatable IT, HR, marketing and financial support with specialist and relationship-heavy professional work.

pi_dist.rationale: A service-line blend retains most near-term savings because tax-firm respondents expected stable-to-higher rates and frequently planned to pass AI costs through, but hourly and benchmarked compliance fees eventually share savings with clients. Attest and complex tax retain more than commodity tax and CAS.
caveats: No public source gives a 541211 revenue-weighted pass-through fraction by attest, tax and CAS; 0.75 is a bounded blend.
Survey expectations may differ from realized pricing once AI production becomes widespread.

pi_moat.rationale: Licensure, independence, recurring client records and accountable advice create switching friction, but simpler tax and bookkeeping work can self-serve and active consolidators compete for clients and staff.
caveats: The attest regulatory floor protects demand but is not treated as full pricing power.
Moat varies sharply with service mix, niche specialization and partner relationship concentration.

t50_years.rationale: Tax/accounting/audit organizational use rose from 8% in 2024 to 21% in early 2025, but effective adoption requires secure data, workflow integration and review controls. A three-year path to 50% effective use is faster than linear continuation but slower than tool availability.
caveats: Observed use is not equivalent to 50% effective task adoption.
The global multi-size survey is a proxy for U.S. lower-middle-market CPA firms.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Thomson Reuters Figure 11 reports 21% of tax, accounting and audit organizations already using GenAI in early 2025.
current_adoption_pct.plausible_range: 0.15-0.30 for U.S. lower-middle-market effective organizational use
caveats: The reported 21% covers multiple countries, organization types and sizes and measures use, not depth or realized labor substitution.

historical_analogs.value: Cloud tax, workflow and document-management adoption: vendor embedding can move firms quickly once confidentiality and review controls become standard, while realization practices slow immediate fee surrender.
historical_analogs.rationale: The report documents a shift from consideration toward organizational rollout and broad use cases, but it is not a quantified historical diffusion study. The analog informs the bounded t50 range rather than supplying a separate numeric operand.
caveats: Qualitative analog; cloud and e-file transitions had different regulation, accuracy and data-security risks.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: The 2025 succession publication states that the 2024 Rosenberg MAP Survey found 22% of CPA-firm partners were over 60.
caveats: Rosenberg participants may skew toward multi-partner firms and do not form a census of all 541211 owners.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: AICPA/PCPS data cited in the publication show 73% expect succession issues in ten years and 57% of firms under $8M have no succession plan.
caveats: The cited succession survey was tabulated in 2020, although the 2024 partner-age evidence is newer.

active_consolidators.rationale: KPMG reports 11 of the 30 largest U.S. accounting firms had PE investment by Q3 2025. I use 11 as a conservative count of visible platform consolidators rather than extrapolating an unverified exhaustive total.
caveats: The count covers PE-backed top-30 firms, not every strategic, regional or independent acquirer.
PE investment does not guarantee that every platform is actively bidding for this exact EBITDA band.

buy_mult.rationale: The AICPA/Aon guide reports 2.99-4.45x average accounting-firm EBITDA, while current transaction analysis reports 4.0-5.5x for small-to-mid-size traditional firms. A $1-10M EBITDA target merits 4.5x after replacing owner compensation with market pay.
caveats: Many practice sales quote revenue or SDE; the selected multiple applies only after market-rate owner compensation and defensible add-backs.
Recurring mix, concentration, audit quality and partner dependence can move pricing materially.

exit_mult.rationale: Current transaction analysis places PE platform accounting acquisitions at 7.0-10.0x+ adjusted EBITDA. The midpoint-to-upper-midpoint 8.5x assumes genuine scale, retained management, diversified clients and normalized owner compensation, not merely passage of time.
caveats: Platform pricing is not assured for a roll-up; integration, quality and service mix must create platform attributes.
The source is transaction-advisory analysis rather than a complete public deal database.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed hourly, hours-anchored fixed fee and increasingly value-priced engagements; attest and complex tax support price, while commodity tax and CAS face benchmarking and self-service pressure.
pricing_structure.rationale: Thomson Reuters reports firm expectations for rates and alternative-fee effects from GenAI; AICPA establishes the distinct regulated attest structure. The blend explicitly separates the statutory attest floor from more contestable tax and CAS pricing.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Near-term stable-to-rising effective rates; medium-term 10-30% fee compression on commodity production if savings are competed away
caveats: No public benchmark supplies a representative 541211 revenue-weighted billing-method split.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The task potential is high but the current 21% organizational use rate and three-year t50 keep near-term realization below steady-state potential; no adoption speed is used to inflate role fractions.

terminal_value.justification: Licensed, independent CPA firms remain necessary for attest work and accountable complex advice even as preparation hours fall. Tax and CAS are more pressured, so durable describes the blended operator rather than every service line.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No undisclosed proxy is treated as direct 541211 evidence. The multi-country tax/accounting/audit adoption survey, four-digit OEWS role mix, top-30 PE platform count and valuation-advisory ranges are disclosed on the affected inputs.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Hourly and hours-anchored fixed fees can transmit productivity to clients, particularly for commodity tax and CAS. pi_dist therefore retains 75%, not 100%, before a separate moat discount; the attest demand floor is not counted as automatic capture.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied occupation mix is four-digit 5412 rather than six-digit 541211 and includes tax-preparation, payroll and other accounting establishments; partner draws are not fully represented in wage data.
sensitivity: A CPA-office-specific mix would likely increase accountants' weight and reduce some clerical roles.
Including market-rate partner labor would raise the economic labor base but add a relatively low-automation licensed/relationship layer.
The selected residual fraction is bounded because nearly 30% of wage share is unlisted.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Mixed tax and attest CPA firm with recurring SMB clients
market_boundary.selection_basis: The Census 541211 definition requires certified-accountant establishments; tax-only 541213, payroll 541214 and other accounting 541219 are outside the target even though the supplied role mix spans their four-digit parent.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Changes A from 5.0 to 2.0; secure workflow integration, not tool awareness, is the key uncertainty.
Changes C proportionally and determines whether labor savings become margin or lower fees.
Materially changes M; platform pricing requires scaled management and diversified revenue.

reviewer_note: Treat multiples only as adjusted EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation. Do not reuse succession, licensing or PE activity as evidence for task replaceability or adoption.
